services.name: Engine Oil & Filter Change
steps: Warm engine 5 min, park level.
Remove 15mm drain plug (passenger side of pan), drain into pan.
Remove oil filter (driver front of pan); confirm old gasket comes off with it.
Oil the new PF48 gasket, thread on hand-tight plus 3/4-1 turn.
Reinstall drain plug, torque to 18 lb-ft.
Fill 6.0 qts 5W-30, start engine, check for leaks, verify dipstick level.
Reset Oil Life: Key ON engine OFF, floor the gas pedal 3x within 5 sec.
services.tips: 6.2L L92 (non-AFM) runs great on 5W-30 High Mileage synthetic even at 160k+ miles. Prefill filter halfway to reduce dry-start wear.
services.whatItDoes: Removes old, contaminated oil and replaces it along with the filter, restoring clean lubrication and cooling to all moving engine parts.
services.whyItMatters: Oil breaks down and picks up metal particles, soot, and acids over time. Skipping this accelerates engine wear, can cause sludge buildup, and is the single most impactful thing you can do for engine longevity.
services.ifDelayed: Old oil thickens/oxidizes into sludge that clogs oil passages and the pickup screen, causing accelerated bearing and cam wear; in severe cases this leads to a spun rod bearing knock and a full engine rebuild costing $4,000-$8,000+ versus a $50 service.

services.name: Engine Air Filter
steps: Locate airbox, passenger side of engine bay.
Loosen 4 captive screws (leave in lid).
Lift lid, vacuum debris from housing.
Insert new filter, seat rubber seal in groove.
Reinstall lid, hand-tighten screws only.
services.tips: Check every oil change in dusty/gravel driveway conditions; replace early if visibly dark.
services.whatItDoes: Replaces the paper/foam filter that cleans air before it enters the engine's intake.
services.whyItMatters: A clogged filter restricts airflow, hurting fuel economy and power, and on turbo engines can increase strain on the turbocharger. Cheap insurance against long-term intake wear.
services.ifDelayed: Restricted airflow richens the fuel mixture, cutting fuel economy 5-10% and causing soot buildup on injectors/spark plugs; on turbo engines it can also increase intake vacuum and pull more debris past the filter edges over time.

services.name: Cabin Air Filter
steps: Remove kick panel under passenger glovebox.
Inspect blower housing for factory-cut slot.
If uncut, carefully knife-cut the molded rectangle.
Slide out old filter (bends around carpet).
Insert new filter, arrows toward rear/blower, snap cover on.
services.tips: 2007-2014 GM full-size trucks shipped WITHOUT a cabin filter from the factory; the Dorman kit is required the first time.
services.whatItDoes: Replaces the filter that cleans air blown through the HVAC system into the passenger cabin.
services.whyItMatters: A clogged cabin filter reduces airflow from vents, causes musty odors, and lets more dust/allergens into the cabin. Not safety-critical but affects comfort and HVAC blower motor lifespan.
services.ifDelayed: A clogged filter starves the HVAC blower, reducing defrost/AC output (a safety issue in fog/rain) and can let the blower motor overheat from working harder against the blockage, shortening motor life.

services.name: Transmission Fluid & Filter (6L80)
steps: Warm the vehicle 5-10 min so fluid flows but stays well below hot (140F+ ATF can cause burns) - aim for warm, not hot.
Raise and support on 4 jack stands at frame rails; this job needs the truck fully level and high enough to slide a drain pan and your arms under the pan.

REAL-WORLD TRICK: On 4WD/AWD Yukon XL, the passenger-side exhaust Y-pipe/mid-pipe runs directly under the transmission pan's rear-right corner. Support the exhaust with a floor jack or ratchet strap, then remove the 2 rubber exhaust hangers just behind the Y-pipe junction and let that section drop/hang - this gives 3-4 inches of extra clearance to angle the pan out without denting it.
If equipped, remove the skid plate (4-6 bolts, 15mm/13mm) for full access to the pan and filter.
Position a low-profile drain pan (fluid will come out at an angle since the pan isn't flat).
Loosen all 10mm pan bolts in a spiral pattern; remove all bolts EXCEPT the 2 front bolts, leaving them started by 2-3 threads so the pan hinges down instead of dropping suddenly.
Gently pry down at a rear corner with a flat prybar to break the gasket seal; fluid will cascade out fast for the first 10-15 seconds - have the pan positioned before you pry.
Once flow slows, remove the final 2 front bolts and walk the pan down and toward the passenger side (where you gained clearance from the dropped exhaust) to clear the crossmember.

Pull the filter straight down firmly; it's pressed into the pump bore and takes real force - confirm the rubber seal ring came out with it and isn't stuck in the pump inlet (very common miss that causes a no-fluid-pressure condition).
Clean the pan and magnet with brake cleaner; a heavy dusting of fine metal fuzz is normal wear, chunks or flakes indicate bigger problems.
Install new filter (push firmly until it seats with an audible/felt click), lightly oil the new pan gasket, and hand-thread all bolts before torquing.
Torque pan bolts to 80-106 in-lb (NOT ft-lb) in a spiral crisscross pattern - overtorquing pan bolts is the #1 cause of pan warping and leaks on this transmission.
Reinstall the exhaust hangers, skid plate, and lower the truck.
Add ~5.5 qts DEXRON-VI through the dipstick tube using a long-neck funnel.
Start engine, let idle in Park 1 min, then cycle through every gear (P-R-N-D-2-1) pausing 3 sec in each, back to Park.
With engine running and at operating temp, check level on dipstick - top off in 0.5 qt increments; overfilling causes foaming and shift issues just as much as underfilling.
Road test 5-10 min and recheck level/leaks once more when fully warm.
services.tips: Filter/fluid service only (not a full flush) - 45k-mile interval extends transmission life significantly on 6.2L trucks. The exhaust-drop trick above is the single biggest time-saver on this specific truck - skipping it means fighting the pan past the crossmember and usually denting the pan edge. Keep a second set of hands or a strap ready since the full pan holds close to 2 qts that let go all at once.
services.whatItDoes: Drains old automatic transmission fluid and replaces the filter, refilling with fresh ATF - includes the shop-proven exhaust clearance trick specific to Yukon XL 4WD/AWD models.
services.whyItMatters: Transmission fluid lubricates and cools internal clutches and gears while also transmitting hydraulic pressure. Degraded fluid causes harsh shifting, overheating, and expensive internal damage.
services.ifDelayed: Delaying past 45k-55k miles lets fluid oxidize and lose friction-modifier additives, causing harsh 1-2 and 2-3 shifts, torque converter shudder at 40-45mph, and eventually burnt clutch material that clogs the filter and starves the valve body of pressure - repair cost jumps from ~$100 (DIY fluid/filter) to $3,000-$4,500 for a rebuilt/replacement 6L80 if neglected long-term.

services.name: Differentials & Transfer Case
steps: Always loosen the FILL plug FIRST on any gear case before touching the drain plug - if the fill plug is seized, stop and address it before draining, or you'll have an empty case you can't refill.

REAL-WORLD TRICK: The front differential fill plug on 4WD Yukons faces straight up but is tucked behind the front driveshaft - use a 3/8" ratchet with a universal-joint adapter to get a straight pull without removing the front shaft.

Transfer case: drain via the lower drain plug into a pan, reinstall drain (torque 15 lb-ft new washer if equipped), refill through the upper fill hole using a hand pump until fluid weeps steadily from the hole.

Front diff: drain, inspect magnetic drain plug for wear metal, refill 75W-90 GL-5 until it weeps from the fill hole - this typically takes longer than expected since the fill hole is small and fluid is viscous.
Rear diff: drain, clean magnet; if there's no drain plug (common on 9.5" 10-bolt rear ends), remove the entire diff cover using a gasket scraper and RTV sealant for reassembly instead of a gasket.
Let all three units sit and settle for 5 minutes after filling, then recheck level - viscous 75W-90 settles slower than engine oil and can appear full when it's actually still low.
services.tips: Severe duty (towing, off-road, water crossings) - cut this interval in half. The universal-joint ratchet trick for the front diff fill plug saves real time versus fighting a straight extension against the driveshaft.
services.whatItDoes: Drains and replaces gear oil in the differential(s) and transfer case that split power between wheels/axles.
services.whyItMatters: Gear oil breaks down under the extreme pressure of meshing gears. Old fluid loses viscosity and lets metal-on-metal contact increase, leading to premature gear and bearing failure — especially costly to repair on AWD/4WD systems.
services.ifDelayed: Degraded gear oil loses film strength under load, letting metal-on-metal contact wear gear teeth and bearings; this often shows up first as whining/howling noise and can require a full differential rebuild ($800-$1,500) instead of a $70 fluid service.

services.name: Coolant / Antifreeze Flush
materials: ~14 Qts Dex-Cool (Orange, GM spec) or approved OAT coolant, 50/50 premix
steps: Cool engine fully. Open radiator drain (petcock) at lower radiator tank.
Remove block drain plugs if accessible for full drain.
Close drains, refill with 50/50 premix through radiator/coolant reservoir.
Squeeze upper radiator hose to help purge air; top off reservoir.
Run engine to operating temp with heater on max; recheck level once cool.
services.tips: Never mix Dex-Cool with green/HOAT coolant - causes gel/clogging.
services.whatItDoes: Drains old antifreeze/coolant and refills the cooling system with fresh coolant at the correct concentration.
services.whyItMatters: Coolant additives that prevent corrosion and control boiling/freezing points deplete over time. Old coolant can corrode the radiator, head gasket sealing surfaces, and water pump — a failure here can strand you and cause engine overheating damage.
services.ifDelayed: Old coolant loses corrosion inhibitors, allowing internal rust/scale buildup that clogs the radiator and heater core and can eat through the water pump seal or head gasket - an overheating failure on the road can crack a head or warp the block, a $2,000-$5,000+ repair.

services.name: Brake Fluid Flush
tools: Brake bleeder kit or pressure bleeder
steps: Top off master cylinder reservoir.
Bleed sequence: RR, LR, RF, LF (typical GM diagonal-split sequence, verify manual).
Pump/hold pedal, open bleeder, close before releasing pedal, repeat until clean fluid flows.
Top off reservoir between wheels; verify firm pedal when done.
services.tips: Brake fluid is hygroscopic - moisture lowers boiling point and can cause spongy pedal/corrosion in ABS module.
services.whatItDoes: Bleeds old, moisture-contaminated brake fluid out of the lines and calipers and replaces it with fresh fluid.
services.whyItMatters: Brake fluid absorbs moisture from the air over time (it's hygroscopic), which lowers its boiling point and can cause a spongy pedal or corrosion inside the ABS module and calipers. This is a direct safety item.
services.ifDelayed: Moisture-saturated brake fluid lowers the boiling point, risking brake fade under hard/repeated braking (e.g. mountain descents or towing), and corrodes ABS module and caliper internals from the inside out - ABS module replacement alone can run $600-$1,200.

services.name: Spark Plugs & Ignition
steps: Remove engine covers/coil packs to access plugs.
Blow out debris from plug wells with compressed air.
Remove old plugs; inspect gap/wear.
Anti-seize threads lightly, install new plugs, torque to 13 lb-ft.
Reinstall coils, apply dielectric grease to boots.
services.tips: L92 iridium plugs are rated for 100k but inspect at 60k if any misfire codes appear.
services.whatItDoes: Removes worn spark plugs and installs new ones that ignite the air/fuel mixture in each cylinder.
services.whyItMatters: Worn plugs cause misfires, rough idle, poor fuel economy, and can eventually damage the ignition coils or catalytic converter if left too long.
services.ifDelayed: Worn plugs misfire under load, which can wash cylinder walls with unburned fuel, damage the catalytic converter (a $1,000-$2,500 repair), and in some cases foul or damage ignition coils that then also need replacement.

services.name: Serpentine Belt & Tensioner
steps: Note routing from underhood sticker/photo.
Rotate tensioner with 15mm breaker bar to release belt tension.
Slip belt off pulleys, inspect tensioner pulley bearing for play/noise.
Route new belt per diagram, release tensioner slowly.
services.tips: Inspect for cracking/glazing every oil change; replace tensioner proactively if it's original at 100k+ miles.
services.whatItDoes: Inspects and replaces the serpentine belt (and/or tensioner) that drives the alternator, power steering pump, and A/C compressor.
services.whyItMatters: A snapped belt instantly kills your alternator charging, power steering, and A/C — often stranding you on the roadside. Cracked or glazed belts are a warning sign that failure is near.
services.ifDelayed: A cracked/glazed belt can snap without warning, instantly killing the alternator (dead battery within minutes), power steering (very heavy steering effort), and A/C - often stranding you and, on some engines, can also drive the water pump, causing sudden overheating.

services.name: Battery Test & Terminal Service
steps: Test resting voltage (12.6V+ healthy) and load test/CCA if available.
Disconnect negative then positive terminal.
Clean posts and clamps with terminal brush; clean corrosion.
Reconnect positive then negative; apply protectant.
services.tips: GM AGM/flooded batteries in trucks typically last 4-5 years in NC heat; test annually after year 3.
services.whatItDoes: Tests battery voltage/capacity and cleans corrosion off the terminals and cable clamps.
services.whyItMatters: Corroded terminals cause hard starts and charging issues, and a weak battery can fail without warning — usually at the worst possible time (cold mornings, after sitting).
services.ifDelayed: A weak, un-tested battery can fail without warning in cold weather or after the vehicle sits, leaving you stranded; corroded terminals also cause intermittent electrical gremlins (dash warning lights, hard starts) that are hard to diagnose after the fact.

services.name: Wiper Blades
steps: Lift wiper arm off windshield.
Press release tab, slide old blade off hook.
Slide new blade on until it clicks.
Lower arm gently onto glass.
services.tips: Replace both blades together even if only one is streaking.
services.whatItDoes: Replaces the rubber wiper blade inserts that clear rain/debris from the windshield.
services.whyItMatters: Worn blades streak and skip, reducing visibility in rain right when you need it most — a low-cost item with outsized safety impact.
services.ifDelayed: Streaking/skipping blades reduce visibility in exactly the conditions where you need it most (rain, road spray), which is a direct safety risk, and can also scratch the windshield glass over extended use.

services.name: Brake Pads & Rotors Inspection
materials: Ceramic pads + rotors when below 3mm/minimum spec
steps: Remove wheel, inspect pad thickness through caliper window.
Measure rotor thickness/check for grooving with calipers.
Inspect caliper slide pins/boots for binding or grease loss.
Repack slide pins with high-temp brake grease as needed.
services.tips: Full-size trucks tow/haul often - inspect more frequently if you tow regularly.
services.whatItDoes: Inspects pad thickness and rotor condition, replacing pads/rotors once they're worn past minimum spec.
services.whyItMatters: Worn-out pads dramatically increase stopping distance and can damage rotors (and eventually calipers) if metal-to-metal contact occurs — a core safety system.
services.ifDelayed: Pads worn to metal backing plates damage rotors (turning a $30 pad job into a $150-$250 pad+rotor job) and dramatically increase stopping distance - continuing to drive on metal-to-metal contact is a direct safety hazard.

services.name: PCV Valve
steps: Locate PCV valve on valve cover or intake manifold.
Detach hose, twist/pull PCV valve out.
Install new valve, reseat hose and clamp.
services.tips: Rough idle or oil consumption increase can indicate a stuck/clogged PCV valve.
services.whatItDoes: Replaces the valve that vents crankcase pressure/vapors back into the intake for combustion.
services.whyItMatters: A stuck or clogged PCV valve can cause oil consumption, rough idle, and increased crankcase pressure that can blow out seals/gaskets over time.
services.ifDelayed: A stuck-closed PCV valve builds excess crankcase pressure that can blow out gaskets/seals (valve cover, front/rear main) leading to oil leaks; a stuck-open valve causes oil to be sucked into the intake, fouling plugs and increasing oil consumption.

services.name: U-Joints & Driveshaft Inspection
steps: Support vehicle safely, grab driveshaft and check for play at U-joints.
Grease U-joints (if equipped with grease fittings) until fresh grease purges seals.
Inspect center support bearing (AWD models) for wobble/noise.
services.tips: Clunking on acceleration/deceleration often points to worn U-joints.
services.whatItDoes: Inspects (and greases, where applicable) U-joints, CV joints, and driveshaft components that transmit power to the wheels.
services.whyItMatters: Worn driveline components cause vibration, clunking, and can fail completely while driving — catching wear early avoids more expensive driveshaft or axle replacement.
services.ifDelayed: Worn U-joints/CV joints that go unaddressed can seize or separate while driving, causing loss of drive power or a dropped driveshaft - a safety hazard and a much larger repair (driveshaft/axle replacement) than the original grease/inspection.

services.name: Wheel Alignment & Tire Rotation
steps: Rotate tires front-to-back (same side) unless directional tread.
Torque lug nuts to 140 lb-ft in star pattern.
Full 4-wheel alignment if uneven wear or after suspension work.
services.tips: Rotate every oil change to maximize tread life on a heavy 4WD truck.
services.whatItDoes: Rotates tires to even out wear and, when needed, adjusts suspension angles so all four wheels track correctly.
services.whyItMatters: Uneven wear from skipped rotations shortens tire life significantly, and poor alignment causes pulling, vibration, and accelerated tire wear — both cost you money in tires over time.
services.ifDelayed: Skipped rotations cause uneven tread wear that shortens tire life by thousands of miles, and unresolved alignment issues cause pulling/vibration and accelerate wear on tie rods and bushings, compounding into a larger suspension repair.
